canonical_name: S̱hayḵh Abú-Turáb, the Imám-Jum‘ih of S̱híráz
the Iṯhná-‘As̱haríyyih (Twelver) sect of S̱hí‘ah Islám
the House of the Báb in S̱híráz

db_statements: it is very likely that Siyyid Káẓim was poisoned in Bag̱hdád by this infamous Najíb Pás̱há who, he says, gave him a potion to drink which caused such intense thirst that it brought about the death of Siyyid Káẓim
Mullá ‘Alí suffered six months’ imprisonment in Bag̱hdád by order of Najíb Pás̱há, the governor of the city

“\[The\] Báb possessed a mild and benignant countenance, his manners were composed and dignified, his eloquence was impressive, and he wrote rapidly and well.” (Lady Sheil’s “Glimpses of Life and Manners in Persia,” p. 178.)
“It was a strange circumstance,” writes Lady Sheil, “that among those who adopted \[the\] Báb’s doctrine there should have been a large number of mullás
According to Samandar (p. 32), S̱hayḵh Abú-Turáb was a native of Is̱hhtihad
According to Samandar, who was one of the early believers of Qazvín (manuscript, p. 15)
According to Ḥájí Mu‘ínu’s-Salṭanih’s narrative (p. 37), the Báb assumed, at the age of twenty, the independent direction of His business affairs
According to Ḥájí Mu‘ínu’s-Salṭanih’s narrative (p. 72), the Báb set out on His pilgrimage to Mecca and Medina
Ḥájí Mírzá Jání was known by the people of Kás̱hán as Ḥájí Mírzá Jáníy-i-Buzurg in order to distinguish him from his namesake

It is related in the ‘Biḥáru’l-Anvár,’ the ‘Aválim,’ and the ‘Yanbú‘’ of Ṣádiq, son of Muḥammad, that he spoke these words: ‘Knowledge is seven and twenty letters.’
he hath declared Knowledge to consist of seven and twenty letters, and regarded all the Prophets, from Adam even unto the ‘Seal,’ as Expounders of only two letters thereof

canonical_name: Áqá Siyyid ‘Alí (maternal uncle of the Báb)
db_statements: “Orphaned at an early age, he was placed under the tutelage of his maternal uncle, Áqá Siyyid ‘Alí, under whose direction he entered the same trade in which his father had been engaged”
He subsequently met Him in Bús̱hihr and stayed for about six months in the same ḵhán in which the Báb and His maternal uncle were residing
the Báb was six or seven years of age when He entered the school of S̱hayḵh ‘Ábid. The school was known by the name of “Qahviyih-Awlíyá.”
“In truth I have become betrothed before the throne of God with Ṣárá, that is to say, the dearly beloved … which signifies that Ṣárá was a Siyyid”
the Báb refers to her in his commentary on the Súrih of Joseph (Súrih of Qarábat)
“Verily, thy son Aḥmad has a refuge in the blessed heaven close to the great Fáṭimih!”
“Glory be to God Who in truth has given to the ‘Delight of the Eyes,’ in her youth, a son who is named Aḥmad.”
At the time of the death of his father, Quddús was still a boy studying in Mas̱hhad in the school of Mírzá Ja‘far.

canonical_name: Áqá Siyyid Muḥammad, the Mujtahid (of Qazvín)
db_statements: When the great Mujtahid Áqá Siyyid Muḥammad arrived at Qazvín, someone asked him if Ḥájí Mullá Ṣáliḥ-i-Baraqání was a Mujtahid. ‘Assuredly,’ replied the Siyyid
Siyyid Muḥammad, having been informed of all these incidents … wrote for him his brevet of Mujtahid
the catastrophe which was fatally to develop the arrogant character of the brother of Mullá Ṣáliḥ … his brother Muḥammad-Taqí, is he also worthy of the sacred title?
One day he went to call upon Ḥájí Mullá Taqí-i-Baraqání who received him apparently with profound respect, but took advantage of the opportunity to ask him some insidious questions
Siyyid Muḥammad had gone to live with one of his colleagues, Ḥájí Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb
While in Qazvín, he sojourned in the house of Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb who was henceforth to be the enemy of the Baraqání family
the S̱háh Fatḥ-‘Alí treated him with great deference, while Áḵhúnd Mullá ‘Alí said of him, ‘He is an ignorant man with a pure heart.’
Then Taqí, calling his youngest brother Ḥájí Mullá ‘Alí, said: ‘Go to my library and bring me the S̱haváhid-i-Rubúbíyyih of Mullá Ṣadrá.’
‘Go to my library and bring me the S̱haváhid-i-Rubúbíyyih of Mullá Ṣadrá.’
Siyyid Ja‘far was unacquainted with the doctrine of the S̱hayḵhís as he was with those of Mullá Ṣadrá.
the Governor of the city, Prince ‘Alí-Naqí Mírzá Ruknu’d-Dawlih, considering the importance of the personages involved in the controversy … resolved to bring about an agreement.
One night, he invited all the celebrated ‘Ulamás of the city to a great banquet.

Her brother ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb-i-Qazvíní who inherited the learning and reputation of his father, himself relates … ‘None of us, her brothers or her cousins dared to speak in her presence, her learning so intimidated us’
Qurratu’l-‘Ayn married, when still quite young, the son of her uncle, Muḥammad-i-Qazvíní who was the Imám-Jum‘ih of the city

canonical_name: Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí (Letter of the Living, martyr of S̱hayḵh Ṭabarsí)
db_statements: Ṭáhirih’s sister, Marḍíyyih, was the wife of Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, who was one of the Letters of the Living, and who suffered martyrdom at Shaykh Ṭabarsí
Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí was the son of Ḥájí Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb, to whom the Báb addressed a Tablet while in the neighbourhood of Qazvín
Ṭáhirih’s sister, Marḍíyyih, was the wife of Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, who was one of the Letters of the Living
Marḍíyyih appears to have recognised and embraced the Message of the Báb

It was at Nís̱hápúr above all, that he made two important conversions in the persons of Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Ḵháliq of Yazd, and of Mullá ‘Alí the Young. The first of these Doctors had been the pupil of S̱hayḵh Aḥmad-i-Aḥsá’í.
The other, a S̱hayḵh like the first, a man of strict ethics and high understanding, held the important position of the principal mujtahid of the city. Both became ardent Bábís.

his father, Siyyid Ja‘far, surnamed Kas̱hfí, was one of the greatest and most celebrated ‘Ulamás of that period. His science had won for him the glorious name of Kas̱hfí, that is to say, one who discovers and explains the divine secrets.
His name was Siyyid Ja‘far, known as Kas̱hfí “the Discloser,” because of his skill in the interpretation of the Qur’án
He became the regular guest of Prince Tahmásp Mírzá, Mu’ayyadu’d-Dawlih, grandson of Fatḥ-‘Alí S̱háh by his father Muḥammad-‘Alí Mírzá.
the regular guest of Prince Tahmásp Mírzá, Mu’ayyadu’d-Dawlih, grandson of Fatḥ-‘Alí S̱háh by his father Muḥammad-‘Alí Mírzá.
Siyyid Yaḥyá “wrote without fear or care a detailed account of his observations to Mírzá Luṭf-‘Alí, the chamberlain, in order that the latter might submit it to the notice of the late king”
